symptoms: Rectangular gray or tan lesions on leaves running parallel to veins. Lesions may show yellow halos. Severe cases cause entire leaves to dry out.
causes: Fungal infection caused by Cercospora zeae-maydis. Favored by high humidity, warm temperatures (25-30 C), and poor air circulation.

severity_indicators.mild: Less than 10% of leaf area affected
severity_indicators.severe: More than 50% of leaf area affected with stalk weakening

treatment.organic: Remove and destroy affected leaves. Apply neem oil spray (5 ml per liter of water) every 7 days.
treatment.chemical: Apply mancozeb or azoxystrobin-based fungicide as labeled. Do not spray within 14 days of harvest.
treatment.cultural: Improve field drainage, increase spacing for airflow, and rotate with non-host crops such as soybean or sunflower.

prevention: Use certified resistant seed varieties, rotate crops, avoid overhead irrigation, and apply preventive fungicide at tasseling.
yield_loss_estimate: Can reduce yield by 30-50% if untreated during grain filling.

symptoms: Ragged holes in leaves, windowing on young leaves, frass in plant whorls, and feeding damage on tassels and cobs.
causes: Larval feeding by fall armyworm moth offspring. Outbreaks increase in warm weather and continuous maize production areas.

severity_indicators.mild: Less than 20% of plants with whorl damage
severity_indicators.moderate: 20-50% of plants damaged with visible larvae
severity_indicators.severe: More than 50% of plants damaged including tassels and ears

treatment.organic: Hand-pick larvae early, use ash or sand in whorls, and apply Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) biopesticide.
treatment.chemical: Use registered insecticides such as emamectin benzoate or lambda-cyhalothrin according to label and safety intervals.
treatment.cultural: Plant early, scout weekly, destroy crop residues, and intercrop with legumes to reduce pest pressure.

prevention: Use pheromone traps, resistant hybrids where available, and keep fields weed-free to reduce egg-laying sites.
yield_loss_estimate: Yield losses can reach 20-70% when control is delayed.

symptoms: Fine yellow streaks along veins, general chlorosis, stunted plants, and poorly filled cobs.
causes: Virus transmitted mainly by leafhoppers (Cicadulina species), especially when young maize is infected.

severity_indicators.mild: Light streaking on a few leaves with little stunting
severity_indicators.moderate: Clear streaking on many leaves with noticeable stunting
severity_indicators.severe: Severe streaking, strong stunting, and poor or absent cob formation

treatment.organic: Rogue heavily infected plants early and remove grassy weeds that host vectors.
treatment.chemical: Use seed treatments or targeted insecticides to suppress leafhopper vectors where locally approved.
treatment.cultural: Plant tolerant varieties, synchronize planting in communities, and avoid late planting near old maize fields.

prevention: Use certified seed, control vector habitats, and maintain field sanitation throughout the season.
yield_loss_estimate: Early severe infections can cause 20-100% yield loss.

symptoms: Lower stalk browning, internal pith discoloration, weak stems, and lodging before harvest.
causes: Fungal pathogens favored by plant stress, moisture extremes, high nitrogen imbalance, and residue carryover.

severity_indicators.mild: Minor basal stalk discoloration with little lodging
severity_indicators.moderate: Rot extends upward with some lodging
severity_indicators.severe: Widespread stalk collapse and major lodging

treatment.organic: Improve soil organic matter and drainage, remove infected residues, and avoid plant stress.
treatment.chemical: Use fungicide-treated seed where available and apply labeled fungicides preventively in high-risk fields.
treatment.cultural: Balance fertilization, reduce plant stress, rotate crops, and harvest promptly once mature.

prevention: Grow resistant hybrids, rotate away from maize, and maintain good nutrient and water management.
yield_loss_estimate: Can cause 10-35% yield loss from lodging and poor grain filling.

symptoms: Dark brown leaf lesions with concentric rings, yellowing around spots, lower leaf drop, and stem or fruit lesions.
causes: Fungal infection by Alternaria solani, spread by rain splash and favored by warm humid conditions.

severity_indicators.moderate: Multiple leaves affected with noticeable defoliation

treatment.organic: Remove infected leaves, mulch soil, and spray copper-based fungicide or compost tea at regular intervals.
treatment.chemical: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin products as labeled.
treatment.cultural: Stake plants, improve spacing, and irrigate at the base to keep foliage dry.

prevention: Use healthy seedlings, rotate with non-solanaceous crops, and sanitize crop residues.
yield_loss_estimate: Can reduce yield by 20-50% in unmanaged outbreaks.

symptoms: Water-soaked lesions, white fungal growth on leaf undersides, stem blight, rapid canopy collapse, and brown firm fruit rot.
causes: Oomycete pathogen Phytophthora infestans, favored by cool wet weather and prolonged leaf wetness.

severity_indicators.moderate: Lesions expanding on leaves and stems
severity_indicators.severe: Rapid full-plant blight with major fruit loss

treatment.organic: Remove infected plants immediately and apply approved copper sprays before spread intensifies.
treatment.chemical: Use systemic and contact fungicides such as metalaxyl combinations according to local label guidance.
treatment.cultural: Increase ventilation, avoid overhead irrigation, and avoid handling wet plants.

prevention: Plant certified seedlings, monitor weather alerts, and spray preventively in high-risk periods.
yield_loss_estimate: Can cause near-total crop failure during severe epidemics.

symptoms: Sudden daytime wilting that becomes permanent, brown vascular tissue, and bacterial ooze from cut stems.
causes: Soil-borne bacterium Ralstonia solanacearum entering roots and blocking water movement.

severity_indicators.severe: More than 50% of plants wilted or dead

treatment.organic: Uproot infected plants with surrounding soil and disinfect tools and irrigation channels.
treatment.chemical: No reliable curative spray exists; focus on soil and vector hygiene practices.
treatment.cultural: Use raised beds, improve drainage, rotate for at least 2-3 seasons, and graft onto tolerant rootstocks where possible.

prevention: Use disease-free seedlings, avoid contaminated water sources, and manage field sanitation strictly.
yield_loss_estimate: Can cause 20-80% loss in warm, poorly drained soils.

symptoms: Dark sunken patch at the blossom end of fruit, tissue collapse, and secondary rot in affected fruits.
causes: Physiological disorder from poor calcium movement, usually due to irregular watering, root stress, or salinity.

severity_indicators.mild: Few fruits with small end lesions

treatment.organic: Maintain even watering and add calcium-rich amendments such as gypsum where soil tests indicate deficiency.
treatment.chemical: Use calcium nitrate foliar feeding or fertigation support as recommended by local agronomists.
treatment.cultural: Mulch soil, avoid root disturbance, and avoid excessive ammonium nitrogen.

prevention: Keep consistent irrigation, monitor soil EC and pH, and use balanced fertility from early flowering.

symptoms: Small white flecks that become orange-brown pustules on leaves, followed by yellowing and early defoliation.
causes: Fungal infection by Uromyces appendiculatus under humid conditions and moderate temperatures.

treatment.organic: Remove heavily infected leaves and apply sulfur-based or neem-based products early.
treatment.chemical: Use triazole or strobilurin fungicides as labeled at first symptoms.
treatment.cultural: Improve airflow with spacing, avoid overhead irrigation, and remove crop residues after harvest.

prevention: Plant resistant varieties and rotate with non-host crops between bean seasons.
yield_loss_estimate: Can reduce yield by 20-60% in susceptible varieties.

symptoms: Poor emergence, yellowing, wilting, root browning, and plant stunting that worsens in wet patches.
causes: Soil-borne fungi such as Fusarium solani and Rhizoctonia solani, especially in compacted or waterlogged soils.

severity_indicators.severe: Large areas with plant death and poor stand

treatment.organic: Improve drainage, apply well-decomposed compost, and use biological seed treatments where available.
treatment.chemical: Use fungicide seed dressing and approved soil fungicides in high-risk fields.
treatment.cultural: Plant on raised beds, avoid overwatering, and rotate crops for multiple seasons.

prevention: Use certified seed, avoid compacted wet fields, and sanitize equipment between plots.
yield_loss_estimate: Can lead to 10-50% loss depending on disease pressure.

symptoms: Angular brown lesions limited by leaf veins, gray center in older spots, and lesions on pods and stems.
causes: Fungal infection by Pseudocercospora griseola spread by seed, rain splash, and infected residues.

severity_indicators.mild: Few angular lesions on lower foliage
severity_indicators.moderate: Lesions across canopy with some defoliation

treatment.organic: Remove infected debris and use copper or botanical sprays where locally validated.
treatment.chemical: Apply recommended fungicides such as mancozeb mixtures at early symptom stages.
treatment.cultural: Use clean seed, rotate crops, and avoid dense canopies that trap humidity.

prevention: Plant tolerant varieties and avoid saving seed from diseased fields.
yield_loss_estimate: Can reduce yield by 15-40% under favorable disease conditions.

symptoms: Leaf chlorosis and vein clearing, brown corky root necrosis, and stem lesions causing poor root quality.
causes: Cassava brown streak viruses spread by infected cuttings and whitefly vectors.

severity_indicators.mild: Light foliar symptoms with minor root discoloration
severity_indicators.moderate: Clear foliar symptoms and moderate root necrosis
severity_indicators.severe: Extensive root necrosis and major marketable root loss

treatment.organic: Rogue infected plants and use only clean cuttings from trusted sources.
treatment.chemical: No direct cure; manage whitefly with targeted insect control where justified.
treatment.cultural: Plant tolerant varieties, sanitize planting material, and coordinate community-level disease management.

prevention: Use certified disease-free stems and monitor nurseries and new fields routinely.
yield_loss_estimate: Can cause 20-70% yield and quality loss.

symptoms: Mosaic leaf mottling, distortion, reduced leaf size, and plant stunting with reduced root formation.
causes: Cassava mosaic begomoviruses transmitted by whiteflies and infected stem cuttings.

severity_indicators.severe: Severe distortion with major growth reduction

treatment.organic: Remove heavily infected plants and replace with healthy clean planting material.
treatment.chemical: No curative chemical treatment exists; vector suppression can reduce spread.
treatment.cultural: Use resistant varieties, clean seed systems, and weed management to limit whitefly habitats.

prevention: Distribute virus-free cuttings and avoid sharing stems from unknown infection status fields.
yield_loss_estimate: Can reduce yield by 20-90% depending on variety and infection timing.

symptoms: Elongated reddish-brown pustules on stems and sheaths, later turning black, with plant weakness and shriveled grain.
causes: Fungal disease caused by Puccinia graminis f. sp. tritici, spread by windborne spores.

severity_indicators.moderate: Pustules on multiple stems and leaves
severity_indicators.severe: Heavy infection with lodging and poor grain fill

treatment.organic: Remove volunteer wheat and nearby alternate hosts where feasible.
treatment.chemical: Spray effective triazole fungicides at early detection following local extension guidance.
treatment.cultural: Use resistant varieties and avoid late planting in high-risk zones.

prevention: Adopt resistant cultivars and monitor regional rust alerts for timely action.
yield_loss_estimate: Can cause 10-70% yield loss during severe epidemics.

symptoms: Yellow linear pustule stripes on leaves, reduced photosynthesis, and early leaf senescence.
causes: Fungal pathogen Puccinia striiformis f. sp. tritici favored by cool humid weather.

severity_indicators.mild: Few stripe lesions on lower leaves
severity_indicators.severe: Severe striping with heavy leaf drying

treatment.organic: Use field sanitation and remove volunteer hosts after harvest.
treatment.chemical: Apply registered fungicides such as tebuconazole or azoxystrobin when symptoms first appear.
treatment.cultural: Plant resistant varieties and avoid overly dense stands.

prevention: Scout frequently in cool wet periods and apply preventive fungicides when regional risk is high.
yield_loss_estimate: Can cause 20-60% yield loss in susceptible fields.

symptoms: White powdery growth on leaves and stems, followed by chlorosis, necrosis, and reduced grain filling.
causes: Fungal infection by Blumeria graminis f. sp. tritici under humid conditions and moderate temperatures.

severity_indicators.mild: Small white patches on lower leaves
severity_indicators.severe: Widespread mildew with premature leaf death

treatment.organic: Use sulfur-based products and improve canopy airflow.
treatment.chemical: Apply labeled fungicides such as triadimefon or myclobutanil where infection pressure is high.
treatment.cultural: Reduce excessive nitrogen, avoid dense planting, and rotate crops.

prevention: Select tolerant varieties and maintain balanced fertility and spacing.
yield_loss_estimate: Can cause 5-30% yield loss when unmanaged.
